reasoning:
I need to think deeply about this, using "nephropyosis" (floating kidney - a condition where the kidney loses its normal anchoring and becomes mobile) as conceptual inspiration.

The key insight from nephropyosis: **the kidney still functions while floating**. It filters blood, produces urine, maintains homeostasis - even when displaced. The problem isn't functional failure but positional instability. Traditional medicine tries to re-anchor it. But what if we designed systems that *work while floating*?

Traditional retirement systems assume a fixed anchor: consistent income → consistent contributions → stable accumulation. For irregular earners, we keep trying to create artificial anchors (automatic deductions, matching, nudges). This is like trying to surgically pin down a kidney that keeps floating free.

**Counterintuitive reframe:** Stop trying to anchor contributions. Let them float. Design a system whose *function* is independent of *positional stability*.

Here's my solution:

```json
{
  "label": "Retrograde Debt Retirement",
  "design_principles": "Accumulate debt instead of savings. Repayment is psychologically easier than saving. Inconsistency is the input, not the enemy. Time-debt accrues automatically; income merely settles it.",
  "core_mechanism": "Everyone accumulates 'retirement debt' at a fixed rate regardless of income. Earnings repay this debt. Zero-income months simply grow the debt. The anchor floats from income to time itself.",
  "how_it_works": "Each month, every participant accrues $400 of retirement debt (adjustable by age/region). This debt compounds at 2%/year. Any income above subsistence threshold triggers automatic debt repayment at 8%. High-earning months aggressively reduce debt; zero-income months just let it grow. At 65, your debt-to-lifetime-accrual ratio determines benefit tier. Ratio below 0.3 = full benefit. 0.3-0.6 = partial. Above 0.6 = minimum floor. Society provides the 'loan' upfront; irregular earners repay when able. Unpaid debt at death is forgiven (not inherited).",
  "what_is_new": "Inverts the saving paradigm entirely. You're not building something—you're settling a debt to your future self that society extended. No required contribution schedule. The system tolerates infinite irregularity.",
  "why_it_works": "Debt repayment activates loss aversion more powerfully than saving activates gain-seeking. Irregular earners already mentally track obligations, not accumulations. The anchor shifts from unstable income to stable time—everyone ages identically. Large windfalls efficiently 'catch up' years of debt in one payment.",
  "why_it_fails": "Creates moral hazard: some will deliberately accumulate debt knowing minimum floor exists. Framing as 'debt' may feel punitive to already-precarious workers. Requires massive public financing for the upfront loan system. Politicians hate anything called 'debt' even when it's beneficial framing.",
  "medium_term": "Gig workers and freelancers stop feeling excluded from retirement planning. Debt-tracking apps gamify repayment. Labor markets tolerate more income volatility without retirement anxiety. Generational wealth gaps slightly narrow.",
  "long_term_vision": "Retirement becomes decoupled from employment stability entirely. The 20th-century assumption that 'good jobs' are the only path to dignified aging dissolves. Society explicitly acknowledges its debt to every citizen's future self, and people repay when their economic kidneys happen to be well-positioned—whenever that is."
}
```

solution.label: Retrograde Debt Retirement
solution.design_principles: Accumulate debt instead of savings. Repayment is psychologically easier than saving. Inconsistency is the input, not the enemy. Time-debt accrues automatically; income merely settles it.
solution.core_mechanism: Everyone accumulates 'retirement debt' at a fixed rate regardless of income. Earnings repay this debt. Zero-income months simply grow the debt. The anchor floats from income to time itself.
solution.how_it_works: Each month, every participant accrues $400 of retirement debt (adjustable by age/region). This debt compounds at 2%/year. Any income above subsistence threshold triggers automatic debt repayment at 8%. High-earning months aggressively reduce debt; zero-income months just let it grow. At 65, your debt-to-lifetime-accrual ratio determines benefit tier. Ratio below 0.3 = full benefit. 0.3-0.6 = partial. Above 0.6 = minimum floor. Society provides the 'loan' upfront; irregular earners repay when able. Unpaid debt at death is forgiven (not inherited).
solution.what_is_new: Inverts the saving paradigm entirely. You're not building something—you're settling a debt to your future self that society extended. No required contribution schedule. The system tolerates infinite irregularity.
solution.why_it_works: Debt repayment activates loss aversion more powerfully than saving activates gain-seeking. Irregular earners already mentally track obligations, not accumulations. The anchor shifts from unstable income to stable time—everyone ages identically. Large windfalls efficiently 'catch up' years of debt in one payment.
solution.why_it_fails: Creates moral hazard: some will deliberately accumulate debt knowing minimum floor exists. Framing as 'debt' may feel punitive to already-precarious workers. Requires massive public financing for the upfront loan system. Politicians hate anything called 'debt' even when it's beneficial framing.
solution.medium_term: Gig workers and freelancers stop feeling excluded from retirement planning. Debt-tracking apps gamify repayment. Labor markets tolerate more income volatility without retirement anxiety. Generational wealth gaps slightly narrow.
solution.long_term_vision: Retirement becomes decoupled from employment stability entirely. The 20th-century assumption that 'good jobs' are the only path to dignified aging dissolves. Society explicitly acknowledges its debt to every citizen's future self, and people repay when their economic kidneys happen to be well-positioned—whenever that is.
